emotive.example: Sleep is an emotive subject for tired parents.

defensive.definition: Protecting yourself because you feel attacked or criticized.
defensive.example: He became defensive when someone questioned his decision.

strategies.definition: Plans or methods for achieving a goal.
strategies.example: They tried different strategies to help the baby sleep.

method.definition: A particular way of doing something.
method.example: This method of teaching works well for young children.

toddler.definition: A young child who has just learned to walk, usually ages 1-3.
toddler.example: The toddler took her first steps across the room.

shame.definition: A painful feeling of embarrassment about something wrong you did.
shame.example: Parents should not feel shame about asking for help.

manipulation.definition: Controlling someone in a clever but unfair way.
manipulation.example: Using tricks to make a child behave is a form of manipulation.

training.definition: Teaching someone to do something through repeated practice.
training.example: Sleep training means teaching a baby to fall asleep alone.

personalities.definition: The special qualities that make each person different.
personalities.example: Twins can have very different personalities.

primary.example: Parents are usually the primary caregivers.

caregivers.definition: People who take care of a child or someone who needs help.
caregivers.example: Caregivers play an important role in a child's life.

conditioning.definition: Training someone to behave a certain way by repeating something.
conditioning.example: Sleep training is a form of conditioning.

reduces.definition: Makes something smaller in amount or number.
reduces.example: This medicine reduces pain within an hour.

contradicts.definition: Says the opposite of what someone else said.
contradicts.example: New research contradicts the earlier findings.

flaws.example: The study had several flaws in its design.

eliminate.definition: To completely remove or get rid of something.
eliminate.example: The new rule will eliminate confusion.

obsession.definition: Thinking about something too much and all the time.
obsession.example: His obsession with cleanliness made life difficult.

interrupted.definition: Stopped in the middle; broken up.
interrupted.example: Interrupted sleep makes parents very tired.

exhausted.definition: Extremely tired, with no energy left.
exhausted.example: After the night feeds, she was completely exhausted.

preoccupation.definition: Thinking about one thing so much that you forget other things.
preoccupation.example: Her preoccupation with work affected her family time.

potential.definition: The possibility of something happening in the future.
potential.example: Every child has the potential to learn and grow.

interfere.definition: To get involved in something in a way that causes problems.
interfere.example: Don't interfere with the child's natural learning.

regulate.definition: To control or manage something so it works properly.
regulate.example: Babies learn to regulate their emotions with help from parents.

internalize.definition: To make something part of your own thinking or behavior.
internalize.example: Children internalize the love they receive from parents.

self-soothe.definition: To calm yourself down without help from others.
self-soothe.example: Babies learn to self-soothe after being comforted many times.

jolt.example: Becoming a parent can be quite a jolt.

associates.definition: Connects one thing with another in your mind.
associates.example: The child associates bedtime with feeling safe and warm.

comfort.definition: A feeling of being relaxed and free from pain or worry.
comfort.example: A soft blanket gives comfort to a baby.

rejection.definition: The act of pushing someone away or not accepting them.
rejection.example: Children fear rejection from their parents.

prioritize.definition: To treat something as more important than other things.
prioritize.example: Parents should prioritize their child's emotional needs.

perceive.definition: To think of something in a certain way; to see or understand.
perceive.example: Children perceive the world differently from adults.

instincts.definition: Natural feelings that tell you what to do without thinking.
instincts.example: Trust your instincts as a parent.

coercive.definition: Using force or pressure to make someone do something.
coercive.example: A coercive cry is hard for parents to ignore.

odds.definition: In 'at odds with': in disagreement or conflict with.
odds.example: Society's expectations can be at odds with children's needs.

biology.definition: The natural processes of living things; how the body works.
biology.example: Biology tells us that babies need close contact.

protracting.definition: Making something last longer than it should.
protracting.example: Forcing independence too early may be protracting the process.

mammals.definition: Animals that feed their babies with milk, including humans.
mammals.example: All mammals sleep close to their young.

weaned.definition: Stopped feeding a baby with breast milk and started giving other food.
weaned.example: The baby was fully weaned by the age of one.

minority.definition: A smaller part of a group; less than half.
minority.example: We are in the minority in thinking babies should sleep alone.

norm.definition: What is considered normal or standard.
norm.example: Co-sleeping is the norm in many cultures.

unheard.example: A child who feels unheard may become sad or angry.

hormone.definition: A natural chemical in your body that controls how it works.
hormone.example: Stress produces a hormone called cortisol.

cortisol.definition: A chemical your body makes when you feel stressed.
cortisol.example: High levels of cortisol can harm a baby's brain.

toxic.example: Being left to cry alone causes toxic stress for babies.

adversely.definition: In a harmful or negative way.
adversely.example: Stress can adversely affect a child's brain development.

wires.definition: Connects and builds pathways (used about the brain).
wires.example: Early experiences affect how the brain wires up.

rupture.definition: A break or damage in a relationship or connection.
rupture.example: Arguments can cause a rupture in your relationship.

repair.definition: To fix something that is broken or damaged.
repair.example: It's important to repair the relationship after a fight.

scold.definition: To speak angrily to someone because they did something wrong.
scold.example: Try not to scold your child when they make mistakes.

alongside.example: Sit alongside your child when they are upset.

empathy.definition: The ability to understand and share another person's feelings.
empathy.example: Show empathy by listening to your child's worries.

altered.example: New parents often have altered sleep patterns.

aroused.definition: Woken up or made more alert.
aroused.example: The noise aroused the baby from a light sleep.

wakefulness.definition: The state of being fully awake.
wakefulness.example: A familiar touch prevents full wakefulness at night.

berate.definition: To criticize someone strongly for a long time.
berate.example: Don't berate yourself for past parenting mistakes.

unscathed.definition: Not harmed or damaged at all.
unscathed.example: Some children survive difficult situations unscathed.

sensitivities.definition: Things that easily affect someone; areas where someone reacts strongly.

resorted.definition: Used something as a last choice when nothing else works.
resorted.example: She resorted to sleep training out of exhaustion.

societal.definition: Related to society and how people live together.
societal.example: There is a lot of societal pressure on new parents.

capitulate.definition: To give up fighting and accept something you don't agree with.
capitulate.example: Many parents capitulate to social pressure.

perpetuating.definition: Causing something to continue for a long time.
perpetuating.example: We risk perpetuating harmful habits from our own childhood.

despair.definition: A feeling of having no hope at all.
despair.example: The child's cries expressed deep despair.

distress.example: The baby showed signs of distress when left alone.

empathize.definition: To understand and share another person's feelings.
empathize.example: Parents who empathize with their children build stronger bonds.

imperceptibly.definition: So slowly that you almost cannot notice it.
imperceptibly.example: Almost imperceptibly, the baby began to cry less each week.

foundation.definition: The base or starting point on which something is built.
foundation.example: Trust is the foundation of a good relationship.

mental.definition: Related to the mind and thinking.
mental.example: Good mental health is important for everyone.

lobbing.definition: Throwing something in a casual, careless way.
lobbing.example: She felt like the teacher was lobbing facts at her.

mercy.definition: Kindness shown to someone, especially when you could be harsh.
mercy.example: The judge showed mercy to the young offender.

co-sleeping.definition: When parents and baby sleep in the same bed or room.
co-sleeping.example: Co-sleeping helps some families get more rest.

abandoned.definition: Left alone with no one to help or care for you.
abandoned.example: A baby left alone at night may feel abandoned.

neuroscientist.definition: A scientist who studies the brain and nervous system.
neuroscientist.example: The neuroscientist discovered new facts about baby brain growth.

nudging.definition: Gently pushing or encouraging someone in a direction.
nudging.example: Sleep nudging is a gentle way to help babies sleep on their own.

pre-verbal.definition: Before a child can speak or use words.
pre-verbal.example: Pre-verbal babies communicate through crying and body language.

recollect.definition: To remember something from the past.
recollect.example: I cannot recollect what happened that night.

tolerable.definition: Able to be accepted or dealt with, not too bad.
tolerable.example: Crying with a comforting parent nearby is tolerable stress.

cycle.definition: A series of events that repeat in the same order.
cycle.example: A typical sleep cycle lasts about ninety minutes.

infant.example: An infant sleeps for shorter periods than an adult.

temper.definition: A tendency to become angry quickly.
temper.example: Don't throw the book away in a temper.
